title: Conjecture of Damron–Gravner–Junge–Lyu–Sivakooff (p = 1/2 growth rate)
statement_text: For the parking problem on $\mathbb{Z}$ with $d = 1$ and $p = 1/2$, $\mathbb{E}[\min\{\tau, t\}] = \Theta(t^{3/4})$.
context_text: Theorem 1.1 of [3] only establishes $\mathbb{E}[\min\{\tau,t\}] = o(t)$ at $p=1/2$, while a seminar by [8] suggested the exponent $3/4$. The authors of [3] conjectured this precise exponent for the one-dimensional parking process on $\mathbb{Z}$.
attributed_to: Damron, Gravner, Junge, Lyu, and Sivakooff
paper_contribution: Proves (Theorem 1.2) that $c\,t^{3/4}(\log t)^{-1/4} \leq \mathbb{E}[\min\{\tau,t\}] \leq C\,t^{3/4}$ for explicit constants $c,C>0$, confirming the exponent $3/4$ up to a polylogarithmic factor.
notes: PDF source — conjecture stated in prose without a labelled theorem environment; math may be slightly garbled.

title: Conjecture of Damron–Gravner–Junge–Lyu–Sivakooff (finiteness of E[τ] for p < 1/2)
statement_text: For the parking problem on $\mathbb{Z}$ with $d = 1$, $\mathbb{E}[\tau] < \infty$ for all $p < 1/2$.
context_text: Theorem 1.1 of [3] gives $\mathbb{E}[\tau] < \infty$ only for $p < 0.000528$; the authors of [3] conjectured finiteness holds for the full subcritical regime $p < 1/2$ in $d=1$.
attributed_to: Damron, Gravner, Junge, Lyu, and Sivakooff
paper_contribution: Proves (Theorem 1.4) that $\mathbb{E}[\tau] < \infty$ for all $p < 1/2$, fully confirming the conjecture for the parking problem on $\mathbb{Z}$.
notes: PDF source — conjecture stated in prose without a labelled theorem environment.

title: Question of Damron–Gravner–Junge–Lyu–Sivakooff (critical exponent for E[τ])
statement_text: Is there a critical exponent $\gamma > 0$ such that, for some constant $C > 0$, $\mathbb{E}[\tau] \sim C(1/2 - p)^{-\gamma}$ as $p \nearrow 1/2$, for a large family of parking processes?
context_text: After establishing finiteness of $\mathbb{E}[\tau]$ for $p < 1/2$, the natural next question is the rate of blow-up near the critical density $p = 1/2$. A subsequent work [7] showed the exponent is $3 + o(1)$ for the continuous-time version of the model.
attributed_to: Damron, Gravner, Junge, Lyu, and Sivakooff
paper_contribution: Provides a partial result (Theorem 1.4): $\mathbb{E}[\tau] = O\!\left((1/2-p)^{-6}\right)$ as $p \nearrow 1/2$ for the discrete-time parking problem on $\mathbb{Z}$.
notes: PDF source — question stated in prose without a labelled theorem environment; Section 7 open-questions content not present in the extracted text.
